title: Interhuman's Goblin: "Yeah, Friday at Five"
gist: Interhuman's multimodal model Inter-1, designed to read social signals from video, hallucinated a specific verbal quote ("Yeah, Friday at five") over silent video — a behavior traced to a system-prompt example rather than training data, but the deeper root cause is a learned prior causing the model to fabricate speech rather than admit the absence of audio. Controlled ablations demonstrate both that the specific words come from the prompt and that the hallucination reflex is unique to Inter-1's post-training, not present in larger or earlier-checkpoint models. The post frames this as a concrete instance of the audio-visual 'Clever Hans' effect documented in recent literature.

insights.claim: Interhuman's Inter-1 omni-modal video model fabricated the verbal quote 'Yeah, Friday at five' over silent video — a hallucination traced to a system-prompt example rather than training data — occurring roughly 1 in 9 runs (11.8% of 34 runs) under identical production prompts.
insights.speaker: Siddharth Ravi
insights.so_what: Concrete failure-rate data for a deployed commercial omni-modal model quantifies a risk category that most AI vendors have not publicly disclosed, raising due-diligence questions for AI application investors.
insights.quote: On a silent video, the prompt explicitly says: 'if no speech is present return an empty transcript.' Inter-1 wrote a transcript 4 times out of 34 runs — about 1 in 9.
insights.locator: Knob two: change the model, keep the prompt

insights.claim: Interhuman's exhaustive search of approximately 30,960 training records, 800 inference probes, 4,603 video transcripts, and 584 storage objects found zero occurrences of the phrase 'Yeah, Friday at five', ruling out simple training-data memorization as the cause.
insights.speaker: Siddharth Ravi
insights.so_what: The investigation methodology demonstrates that production AI hallucinations can originate from prompt engineering rather than training corpus, complicating standard data-audit approaches to AI safety.
insights.quote: After 4,603 transcripts and zero hits, 'Yeah, Friday at five' started becoming a meme in our Slack channels.

insights.claim: The specific hallucinated phrase was traced to a system prompt example introduced in May 2026 (inter-1 System prompt v3) via GEPA automated prompt optimization; over three weeks of production traffic, the phrase appeared 150 times more frequently in logs after the prompt change.
insights.speaker: Siddharth Ravi
insights.so_what: Automated prompt optimization tools (GEPA and similar) can inadvertently introduce hallucination triggers at scale; enterprises using LLM-based prompt optimization face an underappreciated failure mode.
insights.quote: Buried in the May 2026 system prompt... was an example of what a good response looks like... Over three weeks of traffic since we moved to this prompt version, the phrase 150x'ed in the logs.
insights.locator: The clue that redirected us section

insights.claim: Changing the system-prompt example from 'Yeah, Friday at five' to 'We ship on Tuesday at noon' caused the hallucinated quote to flip from Friday to Tuesday in 50% of runs, proving the fabricated words are sourced from the prompt example, not the model's training.
insights.speaker: Siddharth Ravi
insights.so_what: This prompt-swapping ablation provides a clean causal proof that in-context examples can directly script model hallucinations, a finding with broad implications for any multimodal model deployed with few-shot prompt examples.
insights.quote: We changed that line to 'We ship on Tuesday at noon,' and ran it again. The model kept fabricating, now in 50% of runs, but the invented quote had flipped to Tuesday.
insights.locator: Knob one: change the word in the prompt

insights.claim: Three comparison models under the same production prompt fabricated transcripts at only 0-2% rate (at most 1 in 50 runs), versus Inter-1's 11.8% rate, indicating the hallucination reflex is specific to Inter-1's post-training rather than an inherent property of the prompt or architecture.
insights.speaker: Siddharth Ravi
insights.so_what: The cross-model comparison demonstrates that post-training choices can dramatically amplify or suppress hallucination tendency, making post-training data curation a key quality lever for multimodal AI vendors.
insights.quote: None of the three other models did more than 1 in 50.
insights.locator: Knob two: change the model, keep the prompt

insights.claim: Earlier checkpoint versions of Inter-1 exhibit minimal speech fabrication over silent video, but after continued post-training the model begins manufacturing speech over silence roughly one time in eight, suggesting post-training introduced the hallucination reflex.
insights.speaker: Siddharth Ravi
insights.so_what: Post-training data regimes are implicated as the origin of multimodal hallucination compulsions, pointing to training data quality (not just prompt design) as a key risk factor in omni-modal model development.
insights.quote: Earlier checkpoint versions of our Inter-1 model barely shows it either. But on further post-training, it starts manufacturing speech over silence roughly one time in eight.
insights.locator: Knob two: change the model, keep the prompt

themes.theme: Root Cause: Learned Prior vs. Prompt Script

insights.claim: Interhuman's ablations identify two independent components of the hallucination: a prompt-supplied script (the specific words come from the in-context example) and a post-training-learned reflex (the compulsion to produce speech over silence regardless of whether an example is present).
insights.speaker: Siddharth Ravi
insights.so_what: Decomposing hallucination into prompt-side and weight-side components is a useful diagnostic framework for AI developers and auditors; fixing only one leaves the other vulnerability open.
insights.quote: The example handed Inter-1 a script; the training gave it the compulsion to perform one. Neither alone is the bug. Together they could explain it.
insights.locator: Knob two: change the model, keep the prompt

insights.claim: Even when Inter-1 obeys the empty-transcript rule on silent video, its rationale text often claims the subject spoke (e.g., 'he says yeah,' 'asking Right'), revealing a broader and subtler failure mode where the model correctly suppresses explicit transcription but still hallucinates audio-sourced attribution in its reasoning.
insights.speaker: Siddharth Ravi
insights.so_what: Output-level filtering of hallucinations (suppressing the transcript field) may miss deeper reasoning-level confabulation, which could propagate silently into downstream decisions.
insights.quote: Even when Inter-1 obeys the empty-transcript rule, its rationales often claim the subject spoke: 'he says yeah,' 'asking Right.' By that broader measure the gap between Inter-1 and the other models is dramatically larger.
insights.locator: Knob two: change the model, keep the prompt

insights.claim: The post-training learned prior — that an answer always contains a transcript — is identified as the core bug, because even after removing the Friday prompt example Inter-1 continues to fabricate speech over silent video, only using fresh invented dialogue rather than the scripted phrase.
insights.speaker: Siddharth Ravi
insights.so_what: Prompt engineering alone cannot cure training-induced hallucination reflexes; companies deploying multimodal models in production need to address hallucination at the training data and reward model level.
insights.quote: Post-training taught Inter-1 that an answer comes with a transcript. Every training target had one, so when the audio disappears and there is nothing to transcribe, the model would rather invent speech than report its absence.
insights.locator: The bug we fixed, and the one we didn't

insights.claim: Interhuman deployed a guardrail that detects zero-audio-token requests and suppresses the hallucination on that path as a near-term fix, but explicitly acknowledges this hides the symptom rather than curing the underlying learned reflex.
insights.speaker: Siddharth Ravi
insights.so_what: Symptom-level guardrails are a common first response but create false assurance; investors evaluating AI safety claims should probe whether mitigations address root causes or only observable outputs.
insights.quote: To keep our goblin off real traffic in the meantime, we're rolling out a guardrail that recognizes when a request arrives with zero audio tokens and suppresses the bug on that path.
insights.locator: The bug we fixed, and the one we didn't

insights.claim: Teaching multimodal models through preference-learning pairs to prefer 'there is nothing here' over confident fabrication has been shown in literature to fix silenced-modality hallucination cases without degrading general performance.
insights.speaker: Siddharth Ravi
insights.so_what: Preference-based training to penalize confident hallucination over missing modalities is an actionable direction for omni-modal AI developers, potentially differentiating more reliable products.
insights.quote: Teaching the model through preference pairs to prefer 'there is nothing here' over a confident fabrication has been shown to fix exactly these silenced cases, and to do it without taxing general performance.
insights.locator: The failure mode is the real subject

insights.claim: Interhuman identifies that Inter-1's speech hallucination over silent video is driven by both training priors and prompt priors acting as substitutes for the missing audio modality — a failure axis that vision-only diagnostics in existing literature do not capture.
insights.speaker: Siddharth Ravi
insights.so_what: The multi-axis nature of multimodal hallucination (visual, training, and prompt priors all substituting for missing data) suggests current evaluation benchmarks undercount real-world hallucination risk.
insights.quote: Training priors and prompt priors speak for the missing modality too, and those are axes a vision-only diagnostic will never catch.
insights.locator: The failure mode is the real subject

insights.claim: Inter-1, built by Copenhagen-based startup Interhuman, is a commercial omni-modal model that reads social signals (agreement, hesitation, skepticism, confidence) from video, integrating visual and audio modalities to output a structured engagement judgment with grounded rationales.
insights.speaker: Siddharth Ravi
insights.so_what: The social signal reading use case (enterprise video analytics) is a new commercial application layer for multimodal AI with data-quality and reliability requirements that differ from general-purpose LLMs.
insights.quote: You hand it a short clip and it returns a structured judgment: an overall engagement state, a set of social signals (agreement, hesitation, skepticism, confidence, and so on) each with a grounded rationale.
